job_summary: 2,000 sf commercial retail tenant improvement in Phoenix, AZ for sales floor buildout, storefront signage, facade improvements, accessible entrance/ADA path of travel upgrades, and electrical/lighting work. This is a commercial interior/exterior alteration scope that triggers a Building Permit for the TI, plus separate sign and electrical permits as needed. Because Phoenix uses a combined permit system for many trade scopes, the exact permit package should be confirmed in Accela before submission, but this job is not a simple residential-style trade permit.

permits_required.notes: Required for the sales floor buildout, facade improvements, accessible entrance work, and ADA path of travel upgrades. This is the master permit for the commercial alteration scope.

permits_required.permit_type: Electrical Permit — Commercial Lighting and Power Alteration
permits_required.notes: Required for new or modified lighting, circuits, switching, receptacles, and any panel or feeder changes tied to the tenant improvement.

permits_required.permit_type: Sign Permit — Commercial Wall Sign / Storefront Signage
permits_required.notes: Required for storefront signage if the sign is new, replaced, relocated, or altered. Separate from the building TI permit.

permits_required_logic.included_because: The scope includes interior buildout work, facade improvements, and ADA path of travel/accessibility upgrades, all of which are building-code regulated alterations in a commercial occupancy.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Commercial tenant improvement, facade work, and accessible entrance/path of travel upgrades

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Electrical Permit — Commercial Lighting and Power Alteration
permits_required_logic.included_because: The job explicitly includes electrical and lighting work, which requires an electrical permit when circuits, fixtures, controls, or panel/feed changes are added or modified.
permits_required_logic.scope_trigger: Electrical and lighting scope in the tenant improvement

permits_required_logic.permit_type: Sign Permit — Commercial Wall Sign / Storefront Signage
permits_required_logic.included_because: Storefront signage is separately regulated in Phoenix and typically requires its own sign permit even when tied to a tenant improvement.

applying_office: City of Phoenix Planning & Development Department (PDD)
apply_address: 200 W. Washington Street, 2nd Floor, Phoenix, AZ 85003
fee_range: Fee Estimate: $558+ combined permit base for trade-heavy work in Phoenix, plus plan review and sign permit fees; commercial TI projects commonly land around $1,500 - $6,500 in permit fees depending on valuation and scope — verify in https://www.phoenix.gov/administration/departments/pdd/tools-resources/fees.html before quoting

approval_timeline.simple: Over-the-counter or online intake can be same day to 1-3 business days for straightforward commercial submittals if documents are complete
approval_timeline.complex: Plan review commonly runs about 28 days for commercial tenant improvements; express review may be available for qualifying submittals

inspections.description: Inspector checks tenant space layout against approved plans, accessible route widths and slopes, door clearances, entrance threshold details, framing or wall modifications, and that any required fire/life-safety elements are in place before concealment.
inspections.timing: Before drywall, finishes, or concealment of any altered walls, entrances, or accessibility work

inspections.description: Inspector verifies branch-circuit routing, box fill, conductor sizing, fixture rough-in, switching, emergency/egress lighting where required, grounding/bonding, and that any new circuits or panel work match the approved plans.
inspections.timing: Before insulation, ceiling closure, or finish installation

inspections.description: Inspector confirms the sales floor buildout is complete, signage and facade work match the approved permit, accessible entrance and path of travel are usable, lighting operates correctly, required clearances are maintained, and all life-safety items are finished.
inspections.timing: After all work is complete and before occupancy or opening to the public

inspection_booking: Schedule inspections through the Phoenix Accela portal: https://aca-prod.accela.com/PHOENIX. Phoenix requires a minimum 24-hour advance notice; same-day requests are not accepted. Use the permit number in the portal to request each inspection. Phone: (602) 262-7811. Verify available inspection windows in the portal before calling.
license_required: Arizona ROC-licensed contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the tenant or owner. For commercial TI work, the contractor's ROC license number must be on the application. The owner does not typically pull this permit unless specifically allowed by Phoenix and the project qualifies under owner-builder rules.
city_contractor_registration: Phoenix requires a separate City Contractor Registration in addition to the Arizona ROC license. If the contractor registration is expired or not active, the permit application can be rejected. Verify registration status and renewal before submitting any permit application.
what_to_bring: Completed commercial permit application in Accela with contractor ROC and city registration information
Tenant improvement plans showing floor plan, accessibility upgrades, facade changes, and electrical/lighting scope
Arizona ROC license number and Phoenix city contractor registration information
Equipment and fixture cut sheets for lighting, signage, and any electrical devices being installed
Site address, suite number, and tenant contact information
Owner authorization letter or tenant improvement authorization if the contractor is filing on behalf of the tenant
common_mistakes: Submitting the permit application without verifying current Phoenix contractor registration status — an expired or lapsed city registration will block the application silently
Trying to pull only an electrical permit when the scope also includes commercial buildout, facade work, and ADA path of travel upgrades
Forgetting the separate sign permit for storefront signage
Starting facade or accessibility work before the building permit is issued and posted
Under-scoping ADA path of travel work — Phoenix reviewers will look for accessible route, door hardware, thresholds, and clearances, not just the front door itself
pro_tips: Check the Phoenix contractor registration renewal date before starting the application — registration lapses are one of the most common avoidable rejection reasons
Use the Accela portal to submit the full TI package at once; incomplete commercial submittals are what usually trigger the 28-day review cycle
If the storefront sign is separate from the TI, submit the sign permit early so fabrication is not delayed
Bring a clean, coordinated plan set that shows accessibility details clearly — Phoenix reviewers move faster when ADA path of travel is drawn and dimensioned on the same sheets as the tenant layout

code_citation.text: Any owner or authorized agent who intends to construct, enlarge, alter, repair, move, demolish or change the occupancy of a building or structure...

companion_permits.permit_type: Fire Permit — Fire Alarm / Life Safety
companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Fire alarm, emergency lighting, or egress/life-safety systems are altered.
companion_permits.required_if: Fire alarm, emergency lighting, or egress/life-safety systems are altered
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

companion_permits.permit_type: Plumbing Permit — Commercial Fixture or Restroom Alteration
companion_permits.reason: May be required if: Plumbing fixtures, drains, or water lines are added or moved.
companion_permits.required_if: Plumbing fixtures, drains, or water lines are added or moved
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

companion_permits.reason: May be required if: HVAC distribution, exhaust, or equipment is modified.
companion_permits.required_if: HVAC distribution, exhaust, or equipment is modified
companion_permits.requirement_label: May be required based on scope

zoning_hoa_flag: Commercial tenant improvements can still be delayed by zoning, sign code, and historic district rules. Storefront signage may need separate sign review, and facade changes in a historic overlay or special district can require additional approvals. ADA path of travel upgrades may also be reviewed against site access and frontage requirements.
disclaimer: Data sourced Apr 28, 2026. Always verify current requirements directly with your local building department before starting work. Permit fees and requirements change frequently.
confidence_reason: Verified city/trade data found from official sources

_meta.job_type: 2,000 sf commercial retail tenant improvement: sales floor buildout, storefront signage, facade improvements, accessible entrance and ADA path of travel upgrades, electrical and lighting

fee_source.title: Fees, Valuations and Assurances | City of Phoenix

ahj_contact_source.title: Residential Buildings and Permits | City of Phoenix

code_section_source.title: [PDF] Plan Review, Permit, Inspection Criteria for Energy Systems

checklist: Verify your contractor license is current and valid in AZ
Confirm your liability insurance is active (certificate may be required at permit office)
Have the property address and legal parcel number ready
Know the project value/cost — required on most permit applications

Identify altered primary-function area: if scope alters an area where major activity occurs, an accessible path of travel to the altered area is required unless technically infeasible / disproportionate (2010 ADA §202.4; 28 CFR §36.403(a))
20% disproportionality cap: ADA path-of-travel work exceeding 20% of alteration cost is disproportionate at the federal level, but lower-cost items must still be prioritized (28 CFR §36.403(f)(1))

Accessible exterior route: site arrival points + accessible parking/passenger loading + curb ramps + walking surfaces + ramps/landings + slope/cross-slope + door clearances to altered area (2010 ADA §§206.2.1, 206.4, 403, 404, 405, 406)

Restroom path + restroom upgrades: if restrooms serve the altered area, accessible route + door + turning space + lavatory + water closet + grab bars + clearances + signage (2010 ADA §§213, 603-606, 609, 703)
Parking + passenger loading: recalculate accessible-stall count + van space + access aisle + signage for affected parking serving the altered area (2010 ADA §§208, 502, 503; 28 CFR §36.403(e)(1))
Drinking fountains, phones, and signage along the path: tactile/visual signs, room ID, directional, fountain units, public phones if any (2010 ADA §§211, 216, 602, 703, 704)
Doors, thresholds, and hardware on the route: clear width + maneuvering clearance + opening force + thresholds + lever/operable hardware + protruding objects (2010 ADA §§303, 307, 309, 404)
Occupancy load calc per IBC 1004 (mercantile = 60 sf gross/occupant for sales floor; 300 sf for stockroom)
Egress capacity sized to occupant load: 0.2 in/occupant for stairs, 0.15 in/occupant for level egress per IBC 1005
Accessible route from accessible entrance to all customer-accessible areas per ADA §206
ADA fixture counts per IBC Table 2902.1 (mercantile uses lower ratios — verify current edition)
Exterior signage permit (often a SEPARATE permit from the building TI — confirm with city sign code)
Fire alarm strobe coverage per NFPA 72 §18.5 (visible from any point, candela-rated to room area)
Sprinkler hydraulic recalc required if shelving / racking exceeds 12 ft (NFPA 13 §3.3.4 storage occupancy)
Storefront glazing: safety glazing per IBC 2406 for any pane within 24 in of door or 60 in of floor
Demising wall fire-rated per IBC 706.3 + STC ≥40 between tenants if not factory-built
Means of egress: ≥2 exits if occupant load >49 OR floor area >3,000 sf in M occupancy per IBC 1006
Public restroom availability per IBC 2902.3 (required for retail >150 occupants OR >300 ft from public restroom)
Energy code lighting + envelope per IECC C405 / C402 if HVAC or exterior wall is touched
Health department clearance if any food/beverage prep beyond pre-packaged sales

Confirm the required license is active and matches the application: Arizona ROC-licensed contractor pulls the permit on behalf of the tenant or owner. For commercial TI work, the contractor's ROC license number must be on the application. The owner does not typically pull this permit unless specifically allowed by Phoenix and the project qualifies under owner-builder rules.
Bring 2 copies of all documents to City of Phoenix Planning & Development Department (PDD)

rejection_patterns.pattern: Missing Phoenix City Contractor Registration (state ROC not sufficient)

rejection_patterns.fix: Schedule inspections minimum 24 hours in advance through city portal

fee_calculator.note: Provide job_value to calculate an exact fee where formulas are available.

expert_notes.title: Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC) — residential vs commercial vs dual licensing
expert_notes.note: Arizona is unusual in that the ROC issues SEPARATE license classifications for residential, commercial, and dual (both) work — a contractor licensed only for commercial work cannot legally pull a residential permit and vice versa. Verify the license class matches the project type (e.g., B-General Residential Contractor, CR-class residential trade licenses, KB-General Commercial) before contracting; a mismatched class voids lien rights and exposes the contractor to ROC discipline. Check status at the ROC Customer Portal before signing.
expert_notes.applies_to: All licensed construction work in Arizona

expert_notes.title: $1,000 unlicensed-work threshold (handyman exemption)
expert_notes.note: Arizona requires an ROC license for any single project where the combined value of labor and materials exceeds $1,000, OR that requires a building permit regardless of value. Handyman work under $1,000 with no permit required is exempt, but bundling small jobs to stay under the cap is treated as unlicensed contracting. HVAC, electrical, and plumbing work above $1,000 specifically requires the matching trade classification (e.g., CR-39 Air Conditioning & Refrigeration, CR-11 Electrical, CR-37 Plumbing).
expert_notes.applies_to: All paid construction work in Arizona ≥ $1,000 or requiring a permit

expert_notes.title: ARS § 9-835 / § 9-836 municipal permit shot clocks
expert_notes.note: Arizona Revised Statutes 9-835 (administrative completeness) and 9-836 (substantive review) require every municipality to publish licensing time-frames and to complete review within them — typically 30 working days for administrative completeness review and a published substantive review window for residential building permits. Missed deadlines entitle the applicant to a refund of a percentage of fees and can be escalated. Always demand the AHJ's published time-frame document and track the clock from the date the application is deemed administratively complete.
expert_notes.applies_to: All municipal building permit applications in Arizona

expert_notes.title: New 15-day residential plan review requirement
expert_notes.note: A new Arizona state law shortens the substantive review timeline for certain residential permits to 15 working days, accelerating the prior ARS 9-836 framework. AHJs are responding with expedited-review tracks, model-plan libraries, and staff capacity additions. For straightforward SFR/ADU applications using model plans or master-plan certifications, push for the 15-day track and flag any AHJ that quotes a longer turnaround as out of statutory compliance.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential building permits in incorporated Arizona municipalities

expert_notes.title: No statewide building code — 106 separate AHJs
expert_notes.note: Arizona does NOT adopt a statewide building or residential code. Instead, all 15 counties and every incorporated municipality act as independent authorities having jurisdiction (AHJs), producing roughly 106 building-regulating jurisdictions statewide, each adopting their own edition of the IBC/IRC/IECC with local amendments. Always confirm the exact code edition and local amendments for the parcel's jurisdiction before drawing — Phoenix, Tucson, Mesa, and unincorporated Maricopa County can be on different code cycles simultaneously.
expert_notes.applies_to: All Arizona construction — code edition varies by jurisdiction

expert_notes.title: Phoenix 2024 PBCC adoption
expert_notes.note: On June 18, 2025, the Phoenix City Council adopted the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code (PBCC), which is based on the 2024 ICC family of codes including the 2024 IECC for energy. Projects in Phoenix submitted after the effective date must use the 2024 PBCC; projects in adjacent jurisdictions (Scottsdale, Tempe, Glendale, unincorporated Maricopa County) may still be on 2018 or 2021 cycles. Confirm the applicable PBCC edition with Phoenix PDD before plan-set finalization.

expert_notes.title: Tucson / Pima County 2024 code adoption effective Jan 1, 2026
expert_notes.note: Tucson and Pima County jointly adopted updated building codes (2024 IBC/IRC/IECC family with local amendments) that took effect January 1, 2026, after a joint recommendation from the City Manager and Pima County administrators. Permits submitted on or after that date are reviewed under the new codes; applications in process before the cutoff may be grandfathered at the AHJ's discretion. Always confirm which edition applies on the specific submittal date.
expert_notes.applies_to: City of Tucson and Pima County permit applications crossing 2026-01-01

expert_notes.title: Active Management Area (AMA) 100-year Assured Water Supply
expert_notes.note: Arizona law requires residential developers in designated groundwater Active Management Areas (Phoenix, Tucson, Pinal, Prescott, Santa Cruz) to obtain a Certificate of Assured Water Supply (CAWS) proving a 100-year water supply before plats can be approved or building permits issued for new subdivisions. ADWR has been under a moratorium on new groundwater-based CAWS in the Phoenix AMA since June 2023, and a 2026 court ruling further unsettled the rule — confirm CAWS status and whether the project sits inside an AMA before promising a build schedule.
expert_notes.applies_to: New residential subdivisions inside designated AMAs

expert_notes.title: ARS § 11-321 — county building permits and utility preemption
expert_notes.note: ARS 11-321 governs county-issued building permits and explicitly preserves utility providers' separate authority to operate, meaning a county building permit does NOT authorize utility connection or interconnection on its own. The contractor must file a parallel utility application (service connection, meter set, or DG interconnection) with the serving utility — county permit issuance does not waive that step. Always run the county building permit and the utility filing in parallel to avoid a finished build that cannot be energized.

expert_notes.title: APS distributed-generation interconnection — separate filing from building permit
expert_notes.note: Solar PV and battery systems in APS territory require a separate Interconnection Application filed with APS in addition to the AHJ building/electrical permit. APS may request a copy of the AHJ building permit before issuing Permission to Operate (PTO), and the system cannot legally export until PTO is granted. File the APS interconnection package as soon as the design is finalized — do not wait for the building permit, since the two reviews run in parallel and APS turnaround often controls the project's energization date.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential solar PV and battery storage in APS service territory

expert_notes.title: HB 2301 / Mesa solar permit pre-interconnection requirement
expert_notes.note: HB 2301 reshaped Arizona's solar permitting landscape effective 2026, and for Mesa Electric Utility customers specifically, the interconnection application must be received and accepted BEFORE the system is installed (not after as in most jurisdictions). Installing first and applying after is grounds for denial of interconnection. Verify the serving utility's sequencing rule (APS, SRP, TEP, Mesa, and municipal utilities each have their own) before scheduling installation.
expert_notes.applies_to: Residential solar PV in Mesa Electric Utility territory

expert_notes.title: Owner-builder affidavit required on every permit (ARS 11-1605)
expert_notes.note: Per ARS 11-1605, every Arizona building permit requires written documentation of either an Arizona-licensed contractor pulling the permit OR an owner-builder classification signed by the property owner. Owner-builder permits restrict resale within one year (the owner must occupy and cannot have built for sale) and shift all liability to the owner. Counties (e.g., Maricopa) will not accept a permit application without this signed contractor/owner-builder declaration — prepare it before counter intake.

expert_notes.title: Rezoning shot clock — separate from permit shot clock
expert_notes.note: A new Arizona development 'shot clock' law imposes statutory time limits on rezoning decisions, distinct from the ARS 9-835/9-836 permit clocks. Local ordinances (e.g., Prescott Valley 13-14-070) implement parallel administrative-completeness and substantive-review windows for rezoning applications. If a project requires rezoning before permitting, treat it as TWO sequential clocks (rezoning, then permit) — and document each completeness determination in writing to preserve refund/escalation rights.
expert_notes.applies_to: Projects requiring rezoning prior to building permit

expert_notes.title: ROC license filing address and portal
expert_notes.note: ROC license applications, renewals, and complaints are filed via the ROC Customer Portal online, or by mail to Registrar of Contractors, P.O. Box 6748, Phoenix, AZ 85005-6748, or in person at 1700 West Washington Street, Suite 105, Phoenix, AZ 85007-2812. Use the portal for fastest turnaround and to pull license ID cards on demand. Expired or suspended licenses block permit issuance at most AHJ counters — verify status the day of submittal.

hidden_triggers.title: Commercial alteration can require accessible-route/path-of-travel upgrades
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Even when the requested work is not 'an ADA project,' altered primary-function areas can require accessible route, restroom, drinking fountain, parking, and entrance upgrades within the disproportionality cap.

hidden_triggers.title: Lighting changes trigger IECC lighting-power and automatic-control checks
hidden_triggers.why_it_matters: Replacing or reconfiguring lighting can require COMcheck/energy forms, lighting power density compliance, occupancy sensors, daylight controls, and automatic shutoff details.
likely_required_actions: Prepare lighting compliance / COMcheck if required
Show control zones and automatic shutoff

_fee_floor_components.jurisdiction_rationale: baseline calibration from Phoenix restaurant TI test case [verify current Phoenix Planning & Development fees]
